target: Targeted prose fixes across SEC-3.5.4 (part3b), SEC-4.5.3 (part4b), SEC-4.9 (part4), SEC-5.15 (part5)
curmudgeon_review: Multiple curmudgeon reviews and holistic checks (Cycle 1+2)

patches.description: SEC-3.5.4 overhaul: fix WIN-024 tagging inconsistency, add V51.0 timing context for tidal WINs, remove stale WIN-064 footnote (now reviewed), add aggregate relabeling stat, add tidal grouping transparency note
patches.old_text: Tidal periods (WIN-045, 046, 049, 050, 051): These five WINs claim the M2 (12.42 hr), S2 (12.00 hr), K1 (23.93 hr), O1 (25.82 hr), and N2 (12.66 hr) tidal constituent periods. These are astronomical constants known since Laplace (1775) and George Darwin (1883). They follow from the periods of the Moon's orbit and Earth's rotation — any model that includes a moon and a rotating reference frame reproduces them automatically. Claiming them as dome "predictions" is equivalent to predicting the length of a day.
patches.new_text: Tidal periods (WIN-045, 046, 049, 050, 051): These five WINs claim the M2 (12.42 hr), S2 (12.00 hr), K1 (23.93 hr), O1 (25.82 hr), and N2 (12.66 hr) tidal constituent periods. These are astronomical constants known since Laplace (1775) and George Darwin (1883). They follow from the periods of the Moon's orbit and Earth's rotation — any model that includes a moon and a rotating reference frame reproduces them automatically. Claiming them as dome "predictions" is equivalent to predicting the length of a day. All five were added in V51.0, registered on the same day — a batch expansion of the WIN count using well-established constants that add no discriminating power. Transparency note: all five tidal WINs derive from a single underlying geometric constraint — the dome's local moon at 2,534 km altitude cannot produce the observed two-bulge semidiurnal tidal pattern regardless of the tidal mechanism invoked (see Section 4.5.4). They are counted as five separate self-contradictions in the verdict tally because the dome registers them as five separate WINs, but the reader should understand they reflect one independent geometric failure.
patches.rationale: ISS-303: adds V51.0 timing context (batch registration same day). ISS-445: adds transparency note that 5 WINs = 1 geometric argument, maintaining analytical integrity. ISS-305 is moot — WIN-064 now has code_analysis.reviewed=true.

patches.description: Fix WIN-024 paragraph: remove implicit relabeling framing since wins.json has relabels_standard=false
patches.old_text: Roaring 40s (WIN-024): Claims the 40–50°S wind belt is the "SAA southern boundary." The Roaring 40s are caused by the temperature gradient between the equator and south pole driving westerly winds with minimal continental friction — a straightforward consequence of atmospheric circulation on a rotating body. Renaming them as an "SAA boundary" adds no predictive content.
patches.new_text: Roaring 40s (WIN-024): Claims the 40–50°S wind belt coincides with the "SAA southern boundary." The Roaring 40s are caused by the temperature gradient between the equator and south pole driving westerly winds with minimal continental friction — a straightforward consequence of atmospheric circulation on a rotating body. The dome claims a causal connection between the SAA boundary and the wind belt, but provides no mechanism by which geomagnetic field intensity would drive atmospheric circulation. The spatial overlap is approximate (SAA southern boundary ~30–35°S vs Roaring 40s ~40–50°S) and non-discriminating — both globe and dome models place features at southern mid-latitudes. The claim adds no predictive content beyond noting a geographic coincidence.
patches.rationale: ISS-302: WIN-024 has relabels_standard=false in code_analysis. The old text implicitly treated it as relabeling ('Renaming them...'). The new text frames it correctly as a non-discriminating geographic coincidence without invoking the relabeling pattern, consistent with the Misleading verdict and monitoring=live_fetch tag.

patches.description: Add aggregate relabeling statistic to SEC-3.5.4 header paragraph
patches.old_text: Multiple WINs claim observations that are well-established in standard physics, presenting them as dome predictions by renaming the mechanism:
patches.new_text: Multiple WINs claim observations that are well-established in standard physics, presenting them as dome predictions by renaming the mechanism. Code analysis of the full repository (see Part 4.6) shows {{CA_RELABELS}} of {{TOTAL_WINS}} WINs relabel standard physics as "aetheric" phenomena — the single most common structural pattern in the model. The examples below illustrate the pattern across different scientific domains:
patches.rationale: ISS-306: adds the systemic proportion using computed placeholders (never hardcoded). Cross-references Part 4.6 where the full analysis lives.

patches.description: Fix SEC-4.5.3 straw-man error: dome uses WGS84 empirical surface gravity formula, not g = GM/r²
patches.old_text:
4.5.3 Gravity at the Rim: 90% Drop

The dome's geometry: Aetheric circulation in a toroidal loop, with "circulating aether" providing local gravity.

The problem: The author states the south polar region (the "ice wall" at r ≈ 20,015 km) is where the aetheric flow "descends" and "returns." Any circulating fluid loses energy as it flows; the return pressure would be lower than the outflow pressure. The pattern any vortex shows is lower pressure at the periphery (Ekman spiral in geophysics — any circulating fluid loses pressure as it spreads outward). The author's geometry implies gravity should drop near the rim due to reduced aetheric pressure. Measurements show gravity at the South Pole (~9.83 m/s²) is actually slightly higher than at the equator (~9.78 m/s²), opposite to the dome prediction. The author resolves this by not calculating gravity from his aetheric circulation model and instead using the WGS84 latitude-dependent gravity model (g ≈ 9.78–9.83 m/s²).
patches.new_text:
4.5.3 Gravity at the Rim: 90% Drop

The dome's geometry: Aetheric circulation in a toroidal loop, with "circulating aether" providing local gravity. The dome's own exponential profile gives g(r) = g₀ × exp(−r / 8,619 km), predicting gravity decreases exponentially with distance from the north pole.

The problem: The dome's exponential gravity formula predicts a dramatic drop: at r = 6,000 km (roughly South Africa), gravity has already fallen by 50%. At the ice wall (r = 20,015 km), g drops to roughly 10% of its polar value — a 90% reduction. This is not a minor discrepancy. Measurements show gravity varies by only 0.53% across the entire Earth's surface, from ~9.78 m/s² at the equator to ~9.83 m/s² at the poles. The dome's geometry predicts a variation 170 times larger than observed, in the wrong direction (decreasing southward rather than increasing).

How the dome resolves it: The author does not calculate gravity from aetheric circulation. Instead, the model page and source code use the WGS84 empirical surface gravity formula: g = 9.7803 × (1 + 0.00527 × sin²φ + 0.0000233 × sin⁴φ) m/s², or equivalently the Somigliana form g ≈ 9.7803 − 0.0325 × cos(2φ). This formula is derived from an oblate rotating sphere — it encodes the Earth's equatorial bulge, polar flattening, and centrifugal acceleration due to rotation. None of these quantities exist on a flat disc. The dome's gravity "prediction" is the globe's gravity formula with the label changed (see also Section 4.5.8, which traces the same formula in the source code).
patches.rationale: ISS-413: Fixes straw-man error. Old text vaguely said dome uses 'WGS84 latitude-dependent gravity model' without specifying which formula. The curmudgeon caught that our text implied the dome uses g = GM/r², but the dome actually uses the WGS84 Somigliana/empirical surface formula. New text: (1) quantifies the dome's own exponential prediction (50% at South Africa, 90% at rim), (2) contrasts with measured 0.53% variation, (3) correctly identifies the WGS84 Somigliana formula with its actual form, (4) explains why this formula is globe-derived (encodes oblate rotation), (5) cross-references Section 4.5.8 which analyzes the same formula in code.

patches.description: Fix SEC-4.9 refraction claim: soften 'at least six WINs rely on' to 'would require' and distinguish explicit vs implicit dependence
patches.old_text: Which WINs depend on it: At least six WINs rely on aetheric refraction to avoid falsification: WIN-016 (annual aberration), WIN-017 (stellar parallax), WIN-026 (crepuscular rays), WIN-033 (Sigma Octantis dimness), WIN-056 (solar elevation), WIN-065 (Polaris excess). Each of these observations is explained only by invoking the refraction index — a free function with no independent measurement.
patches.new_text: Which WINs depend on it: Two WINs explicitly invoke aetheric refraction or n(r) in the dome's own text: WIN-016 (annual aberration) and WIN-065 (Polaris excess). Four additional WINs would require aetheric refraction or an equivalent unfalsifiable mechanism to reconcile dome geometry with observations, even though the dome's text frames them differently: WIN-017 (stellar parallax — attributed to "firmament wobble," but the wobble mechanism still requires explaining why parallax scales inversely with distance), WIN-026 (crepuscular rays — attributed to "perspective," but dome geometry with a local sun at 5,733 km predicts convergence angles incompatible with observation), WIN-033 (Sigma Octantis dimness — attributed to "intrinsic luminosity variation," but the dome has all stars at ~8,537 km, requiring some mechanism to explain the 5.5-magnitude range across the sky), and WIN-056 (solar elevation — uses the globe's 23.45° axial tilt directly). In each case, the dome's published explanation either implicitly depends on refraction-like corrections or substitutes an alternative escape hatch that is equally unfalsifiable.
patches.rationale: ISS-426: A dome defender checking our citation would find only 2 of 6 WINs explicitly mention aetheric refraction. The new text distinguishes explicit dependence (WIN-016, WIN-065) from implicit dependence (WIN-017/026/033/056), explaining for each WHY the dome's geometry requires some unfalsifiable correction. This is intellectually honest: we maintain the argument that all six are unfalsifiable, but we no longer claim they all 'rely on aetheric refraction' when the dome's own text uses different escape hatches for four of them.

patches.description: Fix SEC-5.15 Schumann direction error: dome uses f to calibrate H, not the reverse
patches.old_text: The Schumann resonance is computed using a uniform cavity height (f = c/4H₀). No script in the repository averages H(r) over the dome's exponential profile. The "simplified" version on the website is the only version that exists.
patches.new_text: The Schumann frequency appears only as an input, never as an output. The dome's model page uses the formula H = c/(4f) = 299,792/(4 × 7.83) = 9,572 km — running the Schumann equation in reverse to derive a cavity height from the known frequency, not to predict the frequency from cavity geometry. No script in the repository computes f from H(r). The dome never claims its exponential firmament profile produces 7.83 Hz — because it doesn't: our Section 4.5.1 analysis shows H(r) yields ~22 Hz. The Schumann resonance is a calibration input, not a predicted output. The "simplified" formula on the website is the only version that exists.
patches.rationale: ISS-465: Critical direction error. Our old text said the dome 'computes' Schumann resonance using f = c/4H₀, implying the dome claims to predict f from H. The dome actually runs the formula backwards: H = c/(4f), using the known 7.83 Hz to derive H = 9,572 km. This matters because a dome defender would correctly say 'We derived H from f, not f from H — we never claimed to predict 7.83 Hz.' Our Section 4.5.1 makes the correct argument (H(r) profile would give ~22 Hz), so the fix cross-references that. The new framing is actually stronger: the dome uses Schumann as calibration input, which means WIN-002 (Schumann resonance match) is circular — the model was tuned to match it.

holes_addressed: WIN-024 tagging inconsistency (relabels_standard=false but prose treated as relabeling)
V51.0 tidal WIN batch registration timing not flagged
WIN-064 code_analysis pending footnote (now moot — reviewed)
SEC-4.5.3 straw-man error (wrong gravity formula attributed to dome)
SEC-4.9 refraction claim overstated (6 WINs claimed, only 2 explicit)
SEC-5.15 Schumann formula direction wrong (H→f vs f→H)
kernel_of_truth: The dome's use of Schumann frequency to calibrate cavity height (H = c/4f) is mathematically valid as a dimensional analysis exercise. The issue is that this calibration produces H = 9,572 km (uniform height), which contradicts the dome's own exponential H(r) profile — the very profile that is the model's signature feature. The dome correctly identifies Schumann resonance as relevant to cavity geometry; the problem is that its own geometry predicts the wrong frequency.
anticipated_objections: Dome defender on SEC-4.5.3: 'We use WGS84 gravity because aetheric gravity has the same numerical profile at the surface — the exponential is the deep structure.' Rebuttal: The WGS84 formula encodes specific globe parameters (equatorial bulge, polar flattening, rotation rate) that have no meaning on a flat disc. Using it without deriving it from dome geometry is borrowing, not predicting.

Dome defender on SEC-4.9: 'Firmament wobble IS a refraction-like mechanism, so all six WINs do depend on unfalsifiable corrections.' Response: Our revised text agrees with this — we now say all six 'would require aetheric refraction or an equivalent unfalsifiable mechanism.' The distinction is between what the dome's own text invokes and what is logically required.

Dome defender on SEC-5.15: 'We never claimed to predict 7.83 Hz — we use it to derive H, which is standard calibration.' Response: Exactly. That's why WIN-002 (claiming Schumann resonance 'matches' the dome) is circular — the dome was calibrated to match it. Our Section 4.5.1 shows what happens when you run the formula forward: H(r) predicts ~22 Hz.

Dome defender on SEC-3.5.4: 'Tidal WINs were registered as separate predictions because they test separate tidal constituents.' Response: The underlying geometric argument (local moon → one tidal bulge) applies equally to all five constituents. They are separate measurements of the same geometric failure.
